title: Rustic X DIY Changing Table
summary: A changing table with X-braced 2x3 side frames, an edge-joined 2x6 top surface and matching middle shelf, a plywood back, and a low topper to keep the pad in place.
description:
Every pocket hole in this build gets planned for which face will actually be seen, which matters more here than on a plain box: the plan is explicit about choosing the "uglier" side of each board to face the inside of the table, since that's where the pocket holes end up. The X-brace in each side frame is built from three pieces rather than two — one full diagonal, and a second diagonal broken into two shorter pieces that lap over and under the first where they cross — which is what makes the X sit flush instead of one piece standing proud where they'd otherwise overlap.

The top surface and the middle shelf are both edge-joined from three 2x6 boards clamped flat and pocket-screwed together before they're ever attached to the frame — gluing and screwing a wide panel together off the table, on a clamped flat surface, gives a truer flat panel than trying to true it up after it's already fastened in place. The exact X-brace lengths are worth scribing directly off your own assembled frame rather than trusting a fixed number, since small variations in how square each frame comes out change that diagonal slightly.

cutList.note: Approximate — scribe and cut to fit directly off your assembled side frame, mitered at both ends.
Approximate — two pieces per frame meet at a lap joint where they cross the long diagonal. Scribe to fit.
Approximate — match to your assembled frame depth.
Forms a low raised end-stop on each end of the top surface.

steps.title: Build the two side frame rectangles
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in each end of the 15" 2x2 rails and screw and glue them to the 31.5" 2x3 uprights, forming two identical rectangular frames. The pocket holes end up on the inside of the changing table, so choose the uglier faces of your boards for this side.

steps.title: Add the X-brace to each frame
steps.body: Attach the long diagonal brace with 2" wood screws driven from the top and bottom, and glue. Screw and glue one short diagonal piece into the upper position with a screw placed slightly off-center where it will cross the long brace. Drill a pocket hole in the remaining short diagonal piece and attach it as the lower half of the second diagonal, using one wood screw and one pocket hole screw, so the two short pieces lap over and under the long brace where they cross.

steps.title: Build the top surface panel
steps.body: Drill evenly spaced pocket holes in the three top-surface boards, choosing the uglier faces for the underside. Clamp the boards flat and screw and glue them together with the top-surface cleats to form one solid panel. A light sanding pass on top once assembled helps even out the joints.

steps.title: Build the middle shelf panel
steps.body: Drill evenly spaced pocket holes in the three middle-shelf boards the same way. Clamp and screw and glue them together, sandwiched directly between the frame's 2x2 rail and 2x3 upright rather than resting on its own cleats.

steps.title: Attach the shelves to the frames
steps.body: Mark where the pocket holes should go so the backs of both shelves sit flush with the backs of the side frames. With the top rails flush with the outside top corners of the side frames, attach with 2" pocket hole screws, checking that both shelves sit square before driving the screws home.

steps.title: Attach the top panel
steps.body: Drill four pocket holes in the back rail (2x3) and attach the top panel with four 2" pocket hole screws through the back rail, plus 2" wood screws driven through the 2x2 cleats.

steps.title: Attach the back panel
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" plywood to 24" x 45-1/2" and nail or screw it to the back of the table. Drawing a line across the back first shows you exactly where to fasten.

steps.title: Build and attach the topper
steps.body: Drill pocket holes at each end of the topper end pieces, adjusting your jig for 1" material, and placing the holes where they'll be best concealed. Assemble the topper with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. If staining, stain both the top panel and the topper before attaching the topper. Attach the finished topper to the top panel with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Give every surface a final sanding and apply your stain or finish of choice.

images.alt: Rustic changing table with X-braced side frames and a low topper
